narrative_blocks.header: Died by suicide on New Year's Day, 1904
narrative_blocks.body: Edward Mansfield of Princeville Illinois died on January 1, 1904 by swallowing London purple, an arsenic-based pesticide widely available to farmers of the era. The Brimfield News January 7 1904 obituary attributed his death to declining health following a runaway accident two years earlier from which he had never fully recovered.

narrative_blocks.header: Returned from California Gold Rush after four years
narrative_blocks.body: In 1849 — at age twenty-two — he left Illinois for the California Gold Rush. He returned to Illinois in 1853 to enter Knox College at Galesburg; the gold years did not make him wealthy but they made him a man who had crossed the continent twice before settling into farming.

narrative_blocks.header: Born in NY during family's CT-to-IL migration
narrative_blocks.body: He was born August 1826 in Schenectady County NY — a temporary stop on the family's migration from Connecticut to Illinois. His parents were on the move; he and his eight older siblings together trace the path of a Connecticut Yankee family relocating westward in the second quarter of the nineteenth century.

narrative_blocks.header: Three of nine children died in infancy
narrative_blocks.body: He and his wife Rebecca Fulton had nine children between 1858 and 1876. Three — George 1862, Richard 1869, and Josephine 1869 — died in infancy or as twin to a sibling who survived. The fifty-percent surviving rate of his infants reflected the brutal mortality of mid-century Illinois prairie childhood.

bio.bio_blurb: Connecticut-born father of an Illinois migration

narrative_blocks.header: Nine children migrated to Illinois and Michigan
narrative_blocks.body: Of his nine documented children with Sarah Sanford, several joined the westward migration of the 1820s-1840s: Eliza Kelley to Elgin Illinois, Henry to Peoria, John to McHenry County, Edward Sr to Princeville, and Sarah Angeline Andrus to Allegan Michigan. The North Haven Connecticut homestead emptied as the family followed the canal and rail routes west.

narrative_blocks.header: He and his wife died the same week
narrative_blocks.body: Late in December 1868 in Illinois, he and his wife Sarah Sanford died three days apart and were buried together at Princeville — far from the Connecticut where he was born and the Stiles homestead of his mother’s family.

sources.label: WikiTree Mansfield-3 (outreach contact for Mansfield descendants)
FindAGrave Sarah Sanford Mansfield (Princeville Township Cemetery)

parents.father_research_notes: Titus Mansfield and Mabel Todd Mansfield of 'Mansfield Farms' New Haven CT. Non-Hooker, not built. Per The Stiles Family in America (BIB008) #452 entry.

burialCemetery.name: Princeville Township Cemetery
burialCemetery.notes: Cemetery in Princeville, Peoria County IL. Burial site of much of the Edward Mansfield Sr family — Leverett Mansfield (HD3919) + wife Sarah Sanford (I00544) + son Edward Sr (HD3988) + Edward's wife Rebecca Fulton + Edward's children Albert, Sanford, Eddie Jr, Joseph, Charles + grandchildren incl Mabel Ann Neill (1935-1938 tragic accident). Primary Mansfield burial site after the family's CT-to-IL migration in the 1830s-50s.
